question: A patient with an intraventricular catheter (Ommaya reservoir) had MRI the day after placement for evaluation. Based on the imaging, which symptom would be most likely?
ground_truth_reasoning:
First, I examine the axial brain MRI image. I see a small focal abnormal signal in the right centrum semiovale / corona radiata region, just lateral to the midline in the superior white matter of the right hemisphere.

Next, I relate that finding to the question, which asks which symptom would be most likely after placement of an intraventricular catheter. A focal lesion in the right cerebral white matter will produce contralateral (left-sided) deficits rather than right-sided deficits.

Then I consider the anatomical location more specifically. The abnormality is high and near the midline within the superior corona radiata/centrum semiovale rather than laterally placed near face/arm cortical projections. Somatotopic organization places lower-extremity motor fibers medially/superiorly in these supracortical white-matter tracts, so a lesion in this medial superior white matter of the right side would preferentially disrupt fibers serving the left leg.

Now I compare this to the answer choices. Options that describe left-sided problems are A (left leg weakness) and B (left facial and arm numbness). Given the lesion’s right-sided, superior-midline white-matter location, a contralateral left leg motor deficit is the best fit. The other choices are either ipsilateral, involve language or visual-field deficits that localize elsewhere, or involve different parts of the body than suggested by the lesion’s position.

Therefore, the imaging supports left leg weakness as the most likely symptom.
caption: FLAIR sequence showing mild hyperintensity in the corona radiata (area surrounding the track of the intraventricular catheter).
sub_caption: FLAIR sequence showing mild hyperintensity in the corona radiata (area surrounding the track of the intraventricular catheter).
image_context: Conventional MRI the following day showed new lesions with mild hyperintensity on FLAIR sequences in the white matter at the right centrum semiovale and corona radiata, with marked restricted diffusion at the same site in the apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) map and extension to juxtacortical frontal white matter and the cingulate gyrus (<xref ref-type="fig" rid="amjcaserep-20-1002-g001">Figures 1</xref><xref ref-type="fig" rid="amjcaserep-20-1002-g002" />––<xref ref-type="fig" rid="amjcaserep-20-1002-g003">3</xref>). The T1 sequence revealed no lesions. These findings were more intense along the track of the Ommaya reservoir without contrast enhancement. The catheter was correctly placed, and there was no liquid reflux surrounding it. HD dexamethasone was initiated owing to an initial suspicion of disease progression. Symptoms resolved during the following 24 hours with no neurological sequelae. He continued his scheduled MTX-containing intraventricular and systemic chemotherapy without further recurrence of symptoms. A control MRI performed 15 days after resolution showed normalization of the restricted diffusion in the ADC map, with more prominent white substance lesions and hyperintensity in FLAIR and T2 and hypointensity in T1. Finally, the patient underwent haploidentical stem cell transplantation with post-transplant myeloablative cyclophosphamide as prophylaxis for GvHD. One year after the onset of symptoms, engraftment has proven successful, and the patient is asymptomatic and in complete remission.). The T1 sequence revealed no lesions. These findings were more intense along the track of the Ommaya reservoir without contrast enhancement. The catheter was correctly placed, and there was no liquid reflux surrounding it. HD dexamethasone was initiated owing to an initial suspicion of disease progression. Symptoms resolved during the following 24 hours with no neurological sequelae. He continued his scheduled MTX-containing intraventricular and systemic chemotherapy without further recurrence of symptoms. A control MRI performed 15 days after resolution showed normalization of the restricted diffusion in the ADC map, with more prominent white substance lesions and hyperintensity in FLAIR and T2 and hypointensity in T1. Finally, the patient underwent haploidentical stem cell transplantation with post-transplant myeloablative cyclophosphamide as prophylaxis for GvHD. One year after the onset of symptoms, engraftment has proven successful, and the patient is asymptomatic and in complete remission. Neurotoxicity has been described in up to 15% of HDMTX courses [4]. Clinical manifestations are various, and patients frequently report transient CNS disturbances that can be significant in some cases. Significant MTX-induced neurotoxicity is rare (only 2–3.8% of patients) [4–7]. Intrathecal administration, and Ommaya reservoirs in particular [8], are often described as risk factors for MTX-induced neurotoxicity. It is noteworthy that the patient we report developed the radiological lesion in the area surrounding the Ommaya catheter (<xref ref-type="fig" rid="amjcaserep-20-1002-g001">Figures 1</xref><xref ref-type="fig" rid="amjcaserep-20-1002-g002" />––<xref ref-type="fig" rid="amjcaserep-20-1002-g003">3</xref>). Two main patterns of CNS symptoms have been described: acute-subacute MTX neurotoxicity with symptoms mimicking strokes, and chronic MTX leukoencephalopathy. Headache, seizures, and acute stroke-like symptoms are the most frequent subacute clinical manifestations of MTX-induced toxicity, with the clinical course ranging from hours to a few days (48–72 hours) and spontaneous resolution without significant sequelae [). Two main patterns of CNS symptoms have been described: acute-subacute MTX neurotoxicity with symptoms mimicking strokes, and chronic MTX leukoencephalopathy. Headache, seizures, and acute stroke-like symptoms are the most frequent subacute clinical manifestations of MTX-induced toxicity, with the clinical course ranging from hours to a few days (48–72 hours) and spontaneous resolution without significant sequelae [4,9]. The hypotheses to explain this clinical behavior include release of adenosine (which impacts vasodilatation with impaired release of neurotransmitters [10]), metabolic derangements associated with folate and homocysteine levels [11], and cytotoxic edema. Nevertheless, in patients with acute/subacute MTX-induced neurotoxicity, repeated doses of MTX do not seem to worsen the symptoms. In the present case, symptoms improved quickly after administration of dexamethasone and did not reappear with subsequent administration of intraventricular chemotherapy. Chronic MTX-related leukoencephalopathy is more common, with series reporting incidences of up to 23% [4]. The disease usually has an asymptomatic course, although the characteristic clinical feature is gradual impairment of cognitive function months after MTX treatment. In many cases, withdrawal of MTX helps to stabilize or improve symptoms, although in some cases it can lead to severe disability [12].
relevant_image_context: Conventional MRI the following day showed new lesions with mild hyperintensity on FLAIR sequences in the white matter at the right centrum semiovale and corona radiata, with marked restricted diffusion at the same site in the apparent diffusion coefficient (ADC) map and extension to juxtacortical frontal white matter and the cingulate gyrus. The T1 sequence revealed no lesions. These findings were more intense along the track of the Ommaya reservoir without contrast enhancement. The catheter was correctly placed, and there was no liquid reflux surrounding it.
